question: Decide which of the following are equal sets and which are not. Justify your answer. A = {x | 3x - 1 = 2} B = {x | x is a natural number but x is neither prime nor composite} C = {x | x ∈ N, x < 2}
answer:
i. Since, A = {x | 3x - 1 = 2} ...(Rule method)
3x - 1 = 2
3x = 3
∴ x = 1
x = {1} ...(Roster Method)
ii. B = {x | x is a natural number but x is neither prime nor composite} ...(Rule method)
B = {1} ...(Listing method)
iii. C = {x | x ∈ N, x < 2} ...(Rule method)
N = {1, 2, 3, 4, .....}
C = {1} ...(Listing method)
From (i), (ii) and (iii)
So, A = B = C
subject: Algebra 9th Mathematics 1

question: Decide whether sets A and B are equal sets. Give reason for your answer. A = Even prime numbers B = {x | 7x − 1 = 13}
answer:
= Even prime numbers
Since 2 is the only even prime number,
A = {2} ...(i)
B = {x | 7x − 1 = 13}
7x − 1 = 13
7x = 13 + 1
7x = 14
x = 14/7
x = 2
∴ B = {2} ...(ii)
∴ The elements in set A and B is identical … [From (i) and (ii)] 
A = B
Hence, A and B are equal sets.
subject: Algebra 9th Mathematics 1

question: Let all the students of a class is an Universal set. Let set A be the students who secure 50% or more marks in Maths. Then write the complement of set A.
answer:
ll the students of a class is an Universal set i.e.
U = set of all the students of a class and
A = set of the students who secure 50% or more marks in Maths 
Now we have to find the complement of A.
The complement of A is represented by A' and it can be calculated as
A' = U - A
A' = All the students in the class obtained less than 50 percent marks.
subject: Algebra 9th Mathematics 1

question: If n(A) = 15, n(A ∪ B) = 29, n(A ∩ B) = 7 then n(B) = ?
answer:
We have,
n(A) = 15, n(A ∪ B) = 29, n(A ∩ B) = 7 
Since, n(B) = n(A ∪ B) + n(A ∩ B) - n(A)
= 29 + 7 - 15
n(B) = 21
subject: Algebra 9th Mathematics 1

question: In a Hostel There Are 125 Students, Out of Which 80 Drink Tea, 60 Drink Coffee and 20 Drink Tea and Coffee Both. Find the Number of Students Who Do Not Drink Tea Or Coffee.
answer:
Let A be the set of students who drink tea, and B be the set of students who drink coffee.
We have,
n(U) = 125, n(A) = 80, n(B) = 60, n(A ∩ B) = 20
Since, 
n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A ∩ B)
= 80 + 60 - 20
n(A ∪ B) = 120
So, the number of students who do not drink tea or coffee = 125 - 120 = 5
subject: Algebra 9th Mathematics 1

question: In a competitive exam 50 students passed in English. 60 students passed in Mathematics. 40 students passed in both the subjects. None of them fail in both the subjects.
answer:
Let A be the set of students who passed in English, and B be the set of students who passed in Mathematics.
We have,
n(A) = 50, n(B) = 60, n(A ∩ B) = 40
Since, 
n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A ∩ B)
= 50 + 60 - 40
n(A ∪ B) = 70 
So, the number of students who passed at least in one of the subjects is 70.
subject: Algebra 9th Mathematics 1

question: A Survey Was Conducted to Know the Hobby of 220 Students of Class Ix. Out of Which 130 Students Informed About Their Hobby as Rock Climbing and 180 Students Informed About Their Hobby as Sky Watching.
answer:
Let A be the set of students who follow the hobby of rock climbing, and B be the set of students who follow the hobby of sky watching.
We have, n(A) = 130, n(B) = 180, n(A ∩ B) = 110, n(U) = 220
Since,
n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A ∩ B)
= 130 + 180 - 110
= 200
So, the number of students who do not have any of the two hobbies
= n(U) - n(A ∪ B)
= 220 - 200
= 20
Also, the number of students who follow the hobby of rock climbing only
= n(A) - n(A ∩ B)
= 130 - 110
= 20
And, the number of students who follow the hobby of sky watching only
= n(B) - n(A ∩ B)
= 180 - 110
= 70
subject: Algebra 9th Mathematics 1

question: Out of 100 persons in a group, 72 persons speak English and 43 persons speak French. Each one out of 100 persons speak at least one language.
answer:
Let A be the set of persons speaking English and B be the set of persons speaking French.
So, n(A) = 72; n(B) = 43; n(A ∪ B) = 100
Now,
n(A) + n(B) = n(A ∪ B) + n(A ∩ B)
⇒ n(A ∩ B) = 72 + 43 - 100
⇒ n(A ∩ B) = 15 
So, the number of person who speak French and English both is 15.
Also,
n(A) = n(A - B) + n(A ∩ B)
⇒ n(A - B) = 72 - 15
⇒ n(A - B) = 57 
So, the number of person who speak only English is 57.
And, 
n(B) = n(B - A) + n(A ∩ B)
⇒ n(B - A) = 43 - 15
⇒ n(B - A) = 28
So, the number of person who speak only French is 28.
subject: Algebra 9th Mathematics 1

question: 70 Trees Were Planted by Parth and 90 Trees Were Planted by Pradnya on the Occasion of Tree Plantation Week.
answer:
Let A be the set of trees planted by Parth and B be the set of trees planted by Pradnya. 
So, n(A) = 70; n(B) = 90; n(A ∩ B) = 25 
n(A ∪ B) =?
Now, n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A ∩ B)
n(A ∪ B) = 70 + 90 - 25
n(A ∪ B) = 135
Hence, the number of trees planted by Parth or Pradnya is 135.
subject: Algebra 9th Mathematics 1

question: If n(A) = 20, n(B) = 28 and n(A ∪ B) = 36 then n(A ∩ B) = ?
answer:
We have, 
n(A) = 20, n(B) = 28 and n(A ∪ B) = 36
n(A ∪ B) = n(A) + n(B) - n(A ∩ B)
36 = 20 + 28 - n(A ∩ B)
36 = 48 - n(A ∩ B)
∴ n(A ∩ B) = 48 - 36
∴ n(A ∩ B ) = 12
subject: Algebra 9th Mathematics 1

question: In a class, 8 students out of 28 have a dog as their pet animal at home, 6 students have a cat as their pet animal.
answer:
Let U be the universe of students in a class, D is the set of dog keepers, and C is the set of students who keep cats.
We have, 
Total number of students = 28; 
n (U) = 28
n (D) = n (dog only) + n (dog and cat)
= 8 + 10 = 18

n (C) = n (Cat only) + n (dog and cat)
= 6 + 10 = 16
n (D ∪ C) = n (D) + n (C) − n (D ∩ C)
= 18 + 16 - 10 
= 34 − 10
= 24 
Students who do not have a pet 
= n (U) − n (D ∪ C)
= 28 − 24
= 4
So, the number of students that do not have a dog or a cat as their pet is 4.
subject: Algebra 9th Mathematics 1

question: Write the subset relations between the following sets. X = set of all quadrilaterals. Y = set of all rhombuses. S = set of all squares. T = set of all parallelograms. V = set of all rectangles.
answer:
Rhombus, squares, parallelograms and rectangles all are quadrilateral.
∴ Y ⊆ X, S ⊆ X, T ⊆ X, V ⊆ X
Every square is a rhombus, parallelogram and rectangle.
∴ S ⊆ Y, S ⊆ T, S ⊆ V
Every rhombus and rectangle is a parallelogram.
∴ Y ⊆ T, V ⊆ T
subject: Algebra 9th Mathematics 1

question: Write the following rational numbers in pq form. 0⋅6∙
answer:
Let x = 0.666.... = \[\ce{0.\overset{\bullet}{6}}\]
∴ 10x = 6.666... = \[\ce{0.\overset{\bullet}{6}}\]
∴ 10x − x = \[\ce{6.\overset{\bullet}{6} - 0.\overset{\bullet}{6}}\]
∴ 9x = 6
∴ x = 6/9
∴ x =2/3 
∴ \[\ce{0.\overset{\bullet}{6}}\] = 2/3
subject: Algebra 9th Mathematics 1

question: Write the following rational number in p/q form. 0.bar37
answer:
Let x = 0.37̅ ...(1)
Multiplying both sides by 100, we get
100x = 37.37̅ ...(2)
Subtracting (1) from (2), we get
∴ 100x - x = 37.37̅ - 0.37̅
∴ 99x = 37 
therefore x = 37/99
So, 0.37̅ = 37/99
subject: Algebra 9th Mathematics 1

question: Write the following rational numbers in p/q form. 3.bar17

Subtracting (1) from (2), we get
∴ 100x - x = 317.17 - 3.17
∴ 99x = 314 
therefore x = 314/99
So, 3.17 = 314/99
subject: Algebra 9th Mathematics 1

question: Write the following rational number in pq form. 2.bar514
answer:
Let x = 2.514̅ ...(1)
Multiplying both sides by 1000, we get 
1000x = 2514.514̅ ...(2)
Subtracting (1) from (2), we get
∴ 1000x – x = 2514.514̅-2.514̅
∴ 999x = 2512
therefore x = 2512/999
So, 2.514̅=2512/999
subject: Algebra 9th Mathematics 1

question: Show that 42 is an irrational number.
answer:
Let us assume that 4√2 is a rational number.
=> 4 √2 = p/q, where p and q are the integers and q ≠ 0.
=> √2 = p/(4q)
Since, p,q and 4 are integers. So, p/(4q) is a rational number.
But this contradicts the fact that √2 is an irrational number.
This contradiction has arisen due to the wrong assumption that 4 √2 is a rational number.
Hence, 4 √2 is an irrational number.
subject: Algebra 9th Mathematics 1

question: Prove that 3 +5 is an irrational number.
answer:
Let us assume that 3 + √5 is a rational number.
=> 3 + √5 = p /q, where p and q are the integers and q ≠0.
=> √5 = p/q - 3 = (p - 3q)/q
Since p, q and 3 are integers. So, (p - 3q)/q is a rational number.
∵ => √5 is an irrational number.
This contradiction has arisen due to the wrong assumption that 3 + √5 is a rational number.
Hence, 3 + √5 is an irrational number.
subject: Algebra 9th Mathematics 1

question: Represent the numbers 5 and 10 on a number line.
answer:
(i) Steps of construction for √5
Step 1: Draw a number line. Mark O as the zero on the number line. 
Step 2: At point A, draw AB ⟂ OA such that AB = 1 unit.
Step 3: With point O as the centre and radius OB, draw an arc intersecting the number line at point P.
Thus, P is the point for √5 on the number line.

(ii) Steps of construction for √10
Step 1: Draw a number line. Mark O as the zero on the number line.
Step 2: At point A, draw AB ⟂ OA such that AB = 1 unit.
Step 3: With point O as the centre and radius OB, draw an arc intersecting the number line at point C.
Thus, C is the point for √10 on the number line.
subject: Algebra 9th Mathematics 1

question: State whether the following number is a surd or not. root4 16
answer:
root (4)(16)
= root (4)(2⁴)
= (2 xx 2 xx 2 xx 2)^(1/4)
= (2⁴)^(1/4)
= 2 ....(Rational number)
So, root (4)(16) is not a surd.
subject: Algebra 9th Mathematics 1
